coredata.dc:title: Critical insight on the hydrothermal effects toward exfoliation of g-C3N4 and simultaneous in-situ deposition of carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wong, Kien Tiek
Jang, Seok Byum
Saravanan, Pichiah
Nah, In Wook
Park, Sehkyu
Choi, Jaeyoung
Park, Chulhwan
Kim, Younghun
Yoon, Yeomin
Jang, Min
coredata.dc:description: In this study, exfoliated g-C3N4 (GCN) coupled carbon quantum dots (CNQDs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal (HT) treatment at various temperatures (100–200 °C) and in various amounts of time (0–20 h). Comprehensive characterization was conducted to study not only the chemical states, photo-optical properties, particle sizes, and crystal structures, but also the effect of these changes on the degradation of BPA. Photocatalytic degradation was conducted under near-visible LED as a low energy light source (0.128 W cm−3). The CNQD that were prepared at 180 °C for 12 h showed the highest degradation rate (3.6 × 10−2 min−1), which was 3.0 times higher than GCN. These improved photocatalytic activities corresponded to increases in the photo-reactive surface via exfoliation of GCN sheets, introduction of heteroatom oxygen onto GCN sheets, the addition of CQDs, and shortened bandgap. These characteristics allow for the effective transfer and separation of electrons. Nonetheless, the structural breakdown of the CNQD was observed when the HT time was longer than 12 h (180 °C). The defected sheets showed a detrimental effect towards photocatalytic degradation by trapping electrons, leading to shorter electron life times.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
Hydrothermal
Carbon quantum dots
Bisphenol A
Exfoliation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of BPA under LED
Physico-chemical analyses of GCN and CNQDs prepared under various HT conditions
Mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of BPA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic degradation of BPA by GCN and CNQDs (A) effect of HT temperature and (B) effect of HT time. Inset graphs are kinetic rate constants for each photocatalyst (LED: 400 nm, BPA concentration: 10 mg L−1, photocatalyst concentration: 0.2 g L−1).
FTIR spectra of GCN and CNQDs (A) effect of HT temperature and (B) effect of HT time.
XPS (A) survey spectra, high resolution spectra of (B) C1s region (C) N1s region and (D) O1s region for GCN, CNQD(180)4, CNQD(180)12 and CNQD(180)20.

(A) XRD pattern and (B) average size distribution for GCN, CNQD(180)4, CNQD(180)12 and CNQD(180)20, (C and D) TEM images of GCN and CNQD(180)12 and (E and F) zoom-in images corresponding to the selected red square area. The marked circles on (F) are CQDs and the inset representing enlarged images of the selected circle.

Photoluminescence emission spectra (A) effect of HT temperature and (B) effect of HT time, (C) time-resolved PL decay spectra, UV–vis DRS (D), of selected photocatalysts, (E) electron spin resonance (ESR) and (F) photocurrent densities for GCN and CNQD(180)12. Insets in (C) show the photographs of CQDs under visible light (left) and UV light (right) and (D) were the calculated bandgap energy.

(A) Photocatalytic degradation of BPA by CNQD(180)12 with addition of different reactive species quenchers and (B) measurement of H2O2 production by KI method (CNQD(180)12 = 0.2 g L−1, BPA = 10 mg L−1).
Schematic of band structure of CNQD(180)12 and BPA degradation mechanism.
Degradation percentages of BPA by CNQD(180)12 for continuous 5 cycles (CNQD(180)12 = 0.2 g L−1, BPA = 10 mg L−1).

head.ce:title: Critical insight on the hydrothermal effects toward exfoliation of g-C3N4 and simultaneous in-situ deposition of carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Kwangwoon University, 20 Kwangwoon-Ro, Nowon-Gu, Seoul 01897, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green City Technology Institute, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Hwarangno 14-gil 5, Seongbuk-Gu, Seoul 02792, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of South Carolina, Columbia, 300 Main Street, SC 29208, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, exfoliated g-C3N4 (GCN) coupled carbon quantum dots (CNQDs) were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal (HT) treatment at various temperatures (100–200 °C) and in various amounts of time (0–20 h). Comprehensive characterization was conducted to study not only the chemical states, photo-optical properties, particle sizes, and crystal structures, but also the effect of these changes on the degradation of BPA. Photocatalytic degradation was conducted under near-visible LED as a low energy light source (0.128 W cm−3). The CNQD that were prepared at 180 °C for 12 h showed the highest degradation rate (3.6 × 10−2 min−1), which was 3.0 times higher than GCN. These improved photocatalytic activities corresponded to increases in the photo-reactive surface via exfoliation of GCN sheets, introduction of heteroatom oxygen onto GCN sheets, the addition of CQDs, and shortened bandgap. These characteristics allow for the effective transfer and separation of electrons. Nonetheless, the structural breakdown of the CNQD was observed when the HT time was longer than 12 h (180 °C). The defected sheets showed a detrimental effect towards photocatalytic degradation by trapping electrons, leading to shorter electron life times.

ce:section.ce:para:
Photocatalytic technology is currently considered one of the most promising technologies for research. This technology is limited to applications in harvesting solar energy, but also plays important roles in various environmental applications, particularly in air and water treatment [1]. The mechanism of photocatalysis involves adsorption of photon energy by the photocatalyst, which triggers the migration of photo-excited electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) [2]. In the excited state, the migration of electron leaves behind holes (h+). The photo-excited electrons are capable of producing highly reactive radicals (
O2 − and
OH) in order to oxidize organic pollutants [3], or the organic molecules can simply be directly oxidized via the highly reactive h+. In most photocatalysts, however, the photogenerated electrons undergo recombination or trapping by undesirable reactants, leading to reduced photocatalytic efficiency.

Among photocatalysts, the metal-free semiconductor graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, referred hereafter as GCN) is widely used in the process of photodegradation and hydrogen evolution, mostly due to its facile and economic synthesis, stability, and suitable bandgap for harvesting solar energy [4]. Nevertheless, GCN suffers from fast recombination and a bulky structure (with a subsequently low surface area), which restrict its photocatalytic efficiency in various applications. In the past years, therefore, a lot of effort has been focused on facilitating the separation of photogenerated electrons in GCN, including through coupling with inorganic semiconductor (TiO2 [5,6], O/Ba [7] and SnO2 [8]), noble metals (Cd, Pt, Pb, Hg and Ag) [9,10] as well as with carbonaceous nanomaterials such as graphene [11,12], graphene oxide [13], and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [14]. Compared to the toxic metallic quantum dots, CQDs, an oxygenous carbon nanoparticle (<10 nm), have received much interest in recent studies [15] due to its benign characteristics, good biocompatibility, high quantum yields, photostability, and high natural abundance [16–19].

In this study, we have synthesized GCN incorporated with CQDs (referred to as CNQDs) via hydrothermal (designated as HT) treatment and have applied the synthesized materials to photocatalysis based on light emitting diodes (LEDs). To obtain efficient and economic photocatalytic degradation, we have attempted to use low energy and near-visible range LEDs (400 nm) instead of conventional mercury lamps. LEDs have distinct advantages compared to mercury lamps including high energy efficiency, constant light intensity, no damages at on/off cycle, and longer lifetime. The life of a LED is estimated to be 20,000–50,000 h, while that of a conventional mercury lamp has 500–2000 h [20,21]. Most importantly, LEDs are viable for tunable radiation wavelength which allows for a high degree of photoreactor design flexibility [22].

Herein, we demonstrate the important parameters in the synthesis of CNQDs, such as temperature (100–180 °C) and time (4–20 h) of HT treatment with simultaneous in-situ growth of CQDs, prior impregnating GCN with carbonized citric acid. Under specific hydrothermal condition, GCN sheets were expected to be oxidized, and henceforth exfoliated due to the reducing strength of hydrogen bond between sheets [23]. First and foremost, in this study we demonstrate photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A (BPA) under the irradiation of LED (400 nm) using various photocatalysts synthesized under specific hydrothermal condition. Then, the possible degradation mechanism was thoroughly investigated via the determination of reactive oxidation species and physio-chemical properties of the prepared materials by various spectroscopic analyses such as Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), particle size distribution (PSD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, time-resolved fluorescence confocal microscopy, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-DRS).

Analytical grade melamine, ammonia, benzoquinone (BQ), sodium azide (NaN3), tert-butanol (TB) and triethanolamine (TEOA), BPA and citric acid were purchased from Daejung chemical. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade acetonitrile was also purchased from Daejung chemical. All chemicals were used without further purification. Potassium biphthalate, potassium iodide (KI), ammonium molybdate (H8MoN2O4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) purchased from Samchun pure chemical were used for H2O2 analyses.

The GCN synthesis method was adopted from Sun et al. [24]. Melamine was calcined at 550 °C for 3 h under atmospheric condition. The obtained yellow solid (GCN) was pulverized into powder using mortar and pestle. As for the preparation of CNQDs, 2 g citric acid was melted in a crucible at 200 °C for 30 min to obtain an orange colored liquid. Then it was neutralized by adding drop-wise into 100 mL ammonia solution (10 mg mL−1). The solution was then dried at 80 °C for overnight. The dried solid (0.05 g) was added in 2 mL DI water and drop-wise onto 1 g GCN in a mortar. The mixture was homogeneously mixed with a pestle and dried for 3 h at 80 °C. And, the final solid was inserted into an autoclave reactor containing 50 mL DI water. The mixture was hydrothermally treated at various temperature and time as listed on Table S1. The obtained products were denoted as CNQD(X)Y where X is the HT temperature and Y is the HT time. CNQD-wo-HT was defined as mixture of GCN and liquefy citric acid without HT treatment. A control was GCN hydrothermally prepared at 180 °C for 12 h without the addition of carbon source and was denoted as GCN(180)12.

In the kinetics of photocatalysis, 500 mL BPA solution (10 mg L−1) was poured in a 500 mL beaker and 0.1 g photocatalyst was added to be 0.2 g L−1. Prior to photocatalytic degradation, the suspension was stirred at 200 ± 50 rpm for 30 min in dark condition to achieve the sorption equilibrium. Then, during the kinetics of photocatalysis, 8 LEDs (400 nm, 8 W per diode) were irradiated to the suspension for 4 h. Energy densities based on surface area and volume were calculated to be 1.27 W cm−2 and 0.128 W cm−3. The distance of suspension surface from LEDs was established with 2 ± 0.5 cm. The experimental setup was depicted in Fig. S1. The homogeneous suspension samples (5 mL) were collected at various intervals for kinetics. By taking off the samples, the suspension level decreased only 10% by the end of experiments. All kinetic tests were duplicated for accuracy. Photolysis of BPA was conducted without addition of photocatalyst. The collected samples were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The pseudo-first order model can be expressed as
(1)
ln
(
q
eq
-
q t
)
= ln
(
q
eq
)
-
k 1
t
where qeq and qt (mg g−1) are the amount of pollutant degraded at equilibrium and at various times t (h), respectively, and k1 (h−1) is the rate constant of the pseudo-first-order model. The values of qeq and k1 can be determined from the intercept and slope of the linear plot of ln(qeq − qt) versus t. The optimal concentration of photocatalyst were determined through different concentration (0.02, 0.1, 0.2 and 1.0 g L−1) of CNQD(180)12.

To investigate the scavenging effect of superoxide (
O2 −) and hydroxyl (
OH) radicals, singlet oxygen (1O2) and h+, the respective scavenging chemicals [BQ (0.001 M), TB (0.01 M), NaN3 (0.002 M) and TEOA (0.01 M)] were separately added into the kinetic batch tests using the BPA solution (10 mg L−1).

The surface functional groups of the prepared materials were determined by FTIR (FT/IR-4600, JASCO) using the KBr pellet method. The oxidation state of each element in GCN and CNQDs was analyzed by XPS (K-Alpha XPS System, Thermo Fisher Scientific) equipped with monochromated Cu Kα (hv = 1486.6 eV) source. The binding energy C1s at 284.6 eV (surface adventitious carbon) was used as reference peak. While the crystal structures were found by XRD (X’Pert Pro, PANalytical) with Cu Kα radiation (k = 1.54051 Å). The PSD was also measured by dynamic light scattering spectroscopy (DLS, ELSZ-2000 series, Otsuka electronic). The surface morphologies and crystal structure were observed by TEM (Tecnai F20, Philips) operating at acceleration voltage at 200 kV. Photoluminescence (PL) data of GCN and CNQDs were obtained by laser raman spectrometer (LabRam HR800 UV–vis µ-Raman, HORIBA Jobin-Yvon Inc.) using 266 nm (30 mW) laser excitation at room temperature. PL decays were recorded on a PicoQuant GmbH, Micro Time-200 Time-Resolved Fluorescence Confocal Microscope under laser excitation at 470 nm (40 MHz) and time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) time resolution is 4 ps. Electron spin resonance (ESR) for various samples was analyzed via JEOL JES-FA-200 with center field at 321 mT. Absorbance and diffuse reflectance spectra were analysed by a UV–vis (Cary G5 spectrophotometer) equipped with DRS (integral sphere) and BaSO4 powder was used as reflectance standard.

Photoelectrochemical measurement was carried out in a standard three electrode configuration with a platinum wire as the counter electrode, Hg/Hg2Cl2 in saturated KCl as the reference electrode, and an indium-doped tin oxide (ITO) conductor glass was used as the working electrode. The electrodes were immersed into 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as electrolyte. Working electrode were prepared via a typical method where 10 mg of GCN or CNQD(180)12 was added into 0.1 mL of Nafion and then uniformly dispersed by ultrasound to obtain a suspension. The suspension was spread onto a 1 × 1 cm ITO glass and allowed to dry for 30 min. Then, it was connected to a copper wire as working electrode. A 3 W LED (365 nm) was used as light source with an energy density of 80 mW cm−2. Ultimately, the transient photocurrent responses of the working electrodes with time (i–t curve) were measured at a 0.5 V bias potential during repeated ON/OFF illumination cycles. Whereas, Mott-Schottky plots were obtained by applying electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at 1000 Hz of frequency under dark condition.

BPA was analyzed by HPLC (Agilent 1200 infinity series) equipped with C18 column (2.1 × 150 mm). The mobile phase consists of ACN and water in a ratio of 40:60 with 0.40 mL min−1. Column temperature was fixed at 35 °C and BPA detection wavelength was at 230 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of BPA under LED
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetics of the photocatalytic degradation of BPA by LED (400 nm) coupled with various photocatalysts are shown at Fig. 1 . Based on the pseudo-first kinetic model, all data had determination coefficients (R2) of >0.98 (Table S2). Fig. 1A presents the kinetics of the photocatalytic degradation of BPA by GCN or various CNQDs prepared at different HT temperatures (100–200 °C). During the dark phase (30 min), BPA was negligibly adsorbed by the photocatalysts. Once the LED was switched on, BPA was gradually removed. It was notably found that not only did CNQDs have better performance than GCN, but also that CNQDs treated with higher HT temperatures had higher removal rates. The degradation rates of BPA by CNQD-wo-HT, CNQD(100)12, −(120)12, and −(140)12 had slight enhancements compared to GCN. However, as the HT temperature increased to 160 °C and above, an exponential increase in BPA degradation rate was observed. CNQD(180)12 had the highest degradation rate (3.6 × 10−2 min−1), which was 3.0-times higher than that of GCN (1.2 × 10−2 min−1) (Fig. 1A inset). When temperature further increased to 200 °C, the kinetic rate decreases to 2.5 × 10−2 min−1.

With the observed optimal HT temperature (180 °C), the effects of HT time (4–20 h) were investigated. Fig. 1B shows that the degradation rates of BPA increased as HT time increased up to 12 h. They decreased, however, when the HT time was longer than 12 h (i.e. 16 and 20 h) (Fig. 1B inset). Compared to CNQD(180)12, the degradation rates for CNQD(180)16 and −20 were 2.6 × 10−2 min−1 and 2.0 × 10−2 min−1, respectively. Additionally, in order to prove the effect of CQD incorporation on the photocatalytic degradation of BPA, the kinetic results for GCN(180)12 were compared to those for CNQD(180)12 (Fig. S2). CNQD(180)12 had a 1.5-times higher degradation rate compared to GCN(180)12 (k1 = 2.4 × 10−2 min−1). The degradation of BPA by photolysis as shown in Fig. S2 were almost negligible.

Overall, the optimal condition of HT treatment for synthesizing CNQD was determined to be 180 °C and 12 h. Accordingly, we established a hypothesis that the various photocatalytic capacities of materials might be strongly influenced by the physico-chemical properties of the photocatalysts used. In the following section, we attempted to prove this hypothesis using several spectroscopic analyses and to elucidate the mechanism of photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physico-chemical analyses of GCN and CNQDs prepared under various HT conditions
ce:section.ce:para: A series of comprehensive spectroscopic analyses were conducted to understand the photocatalytic behaviors of GCN and CNQDs. The changes of surface functional groups in GCN and CNQDs was observed through FTIR (Fig. 2 A and B). The FTIR analyses indicated that the oxidation of CNQDs occurred at a certain temperature and time of HT treatment. For the HT temperature effect (Fig. 2A), there were not any significant changes compared to GCN when the HT temperature was below 140 °C. However, as the temperature raised to 180 °C, new peaks at 1090 and 1738 cm−1 were formed that represented CO and CO stretching [25], respectively. As for the time effect depicted in Fig. 2B, the signals for CO and CO became more intense as time increased from 8 to 20 h. However, the CO group was not observed on CNQD(180)4, possibly due to insufficient time for surface oxidation. All synthesized photocatalysts had typical peaks between 1200 and 1650 cm−1 of wavelength. Specifically, peaks at 1237, 1318, 1405, 1452, and 1578 cm−1 can be ascribed as tri-s-triazine skeleton structure [26]. Other common peaks of GCN observed at 810 and 880 cm−1 were attributed to the bending of the triazine ring, which indicates the existence of a basic melon unit (NH/NH2 group) [27,28]. The peak at 810 cm−1, however, was not presented when the time of HT treatment was either 16 h or 20 h at 180 °C, while the sharp peak at 880 cm−1 was transformed to a broad peak under the same conditions. A new peak at 765 cm−1 did emerge under these conditions, suggesting the formation of hydrogen bonding NH⋯O and NH⋯N, which are typical complexations between melamine and cyanuric acid, causing the triazine ring vibration to shift to a lower frequency [29]. Furthermore, as the HT time increased beyond 12 h, the peaks corresponding to tri-s-triazine skeleton structure (CN and CN heterocycles) appeared much weaker. Thus, at 180 °C, more than 12 h of HT treatment can deconstruct the melon structure of GCN into cyanuric acid via oxidation [30]. The huge differences in CO intensities between 12 h and 16/20 h also hinted at the formation of cyanuric acid. Three highly defined broad spectrums between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 indicated NH stretching vibrations caused by a triketo structure (the most stable form of cyanuric acid) interacting with 3 hydrogen bonds [31,32]. The peaks observed at 2740 and 2855 cm−1 in Fig. S3 corresponded to the skeletal vibration of CH in sp2 and sp3 hybridization, indicating the formation of CQDs [33]. These indicated peaks were not observed on GCN and GCN(180)12.

The chemical composition and oxidation speciation of the samples was further determined by XPS analysis (Fig. 3 ). For this test, the samples selected [GCN, CNQD(180)4, −12, and −20] were chosen because they showed significant structural changes from FTIR. As shown in Fig. 3A, there were not any shifts in binding energies of major elements after adding CQDs and HT treatment. Three obvious peaks were detected at 288, 398, and 532 eV, which indicate C1s, N1s, and O1s signals, respectively [34]. The quantitative analysis of the atomic ratio for carbon to nitrogen (C/N) was tabulated on Table S3, where it can be seen that GCN(180)12 had a higher C/N ratio (0.92) compared to GCN (0.83), indicating that N atoms were being removed from GCN during HT treatment. Moreover, the increase in O atoms indicates the occurrence of thermal oxidation. To obtain detailed information on their chemical compositions, these elements were observed under high resolution narrow scans of C1s, N1s and O1s. The deconvoluted peaks for C1s in Fig. 3B were 3 dominant peaks centralized at 284.6, 288, and 288.9 eV. The peak at 284.6 eV was attributed to the sp2 CC bonds while the peak at 288 eV was due to sp2 hybridized NCN [35]. When HT treatment time increased at 180 °C, the peak intensities at 288.9 eV increased proportionally, representing the higher formation of CO, CO, or COOH [36,37]. However, this peak (288.9 eV) was shifted to 289.3 eV for CNQD(180)20, likely due to the formation of hydrogen bonds with amine groups. A minor peak at 286.4 eV was attributed to CNH2 at the edge of GCN [38], which was mostly removed for CNQD(180)20 as shown. Thus, it appeared that the CNH2 bonds were oxidized into NH3 (g) during HT treatment and that the remaining carbon was transformed into CO/CO [36]. The N1s (Fig. 3C) signals for all samples can be deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies of 398.5, 400.1, and 401.2 eV, which represent CNC (sp2 hybridized nitrogen), tertiary nitrogen N(C)3, and CNH bond, respectively [39]. Similar to the NCN peak, the relative intensity for the CNC peak decreased after HT treatment for 20 h, indicating that some of the tri-s-triazine units of GCN were broken down during this process. Conversely, after 20 h of HT treatment, the N(C)3 peak increased and shifted to 399.7 eV. This implied the oxidation of N(C)3 into CNO. The O1s signal increased significantly for two reasons: the oxidation of GCN by HT treatment and the formation of CQDs. The binding energy of O1s shown at 531.9 eV corresponded to CO (Fig. 3D) [40]. This peak increased when the HT treatment time was 12 h and further increased two-fold when the treatment time was 20 h. The increase of CO corresponded well with the results of FTIR. The binding energy at 533.4 eV was associated with CO/COOH [41]. The COOH in particular could come from the CQDs. A peak in the O1s spectrum positioned at 530.7 eV was attributed to H2O [42].

Fig. 4 A shows the XRD patterns of GCN, CNQD(180)4, −12, and −20. Among these materials, GCN had two major peaks at 13.1° (1 0 0) and 37.45° (0 0 2) which corresponded to the in-plane repeat of the tri-s-triazine unit and the interlayer stacking with a distance of 0.326 nm, respectively [43,44]. With a HT treatment time of 12 h and above, the (1 0 0) signal was less pronounced, indicating a decrease of GCN planar size due to thermal oxidation. Meanwhile, the appearance of peak at 12.2° was transitioned from (1 0 0) signal where the diffraction peak appeared to be more intense and shifted to lower degree [45]. This observation is due the addition of O atoms (oxidation of GCN sheets) onto the lone-pair electrons of nitrogen in the “nitrogen pots” leading to intraplanar separation. The resultant for this phenomenon could be attributed to the oxidation of N(C)3, which is an important linker for tri-s-triazine. Similar result was reported by Sano et al. [46]. In addition, Zhang et al. [47] reported the intraplanar of ordered tri-s-triazine were enlarged by the presence of ZnIn2S4 indicated by the shifting of (1 0 0) signal to lower degree. Aside from these findings, the extended HT time from 12 to 20 h reduces the peak at 12.2 in favor of new peaks at 10.67°, 18.48°, and 21.41°. These peaks were found to have formed to support the formation of the in-plane hexagonal pattern of melamine-cyanurate complexation [44] due to destruction of GCN core structure which is well corresponded with the findings of FTIR analysis. Compared to the bulk GCN, the (0 0 2) peak of CNQDs shifted from 27.45° to 27.67°, signifying denser stacking and a shortening of interlayer spacing, which evidently produces a sharper peak [48]. This observation is most likely due to the exfoliation of GCN at a prolonged time of HT treatment, creating mono- or few-layered GCN [49]. As the control, the XRD crystallography of GCN(180)12 shown in Fig. S4 exhibited identical peaks of GCN(180)12, particularly the shifting of the main peak to 27.69°, indicating exfoliation.

The PSDs of GCN and CNQDs were measured using a Zetasizer (Fig. 4B). GCN had an average size of 580 nm, but CNQD(180)4 showed a 50% reduction in size (to about 280 nm). Prolonging the HT treatment to 12 h further reduced the size of the material to about 100 nm, but there were no significant changes at 20 h. This observed size reduction could possibly be caused by exfoliation. Accordingly, this means that the sheets of GCN could be fully exfoliated with 12 h of HT treatment at 180 °C, but further oxidation on GCN frameworks happened at 20 h, as described by XRD and XPS.

The TEM micrographs in Fig. 4C−F show the physical morphologies of both GCN and CNQD(180)12. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, the GCN sheets were observed to be darker and larger than CNQD(180)12. This prove that the exfoliation occurred during the HT treatment, corresponding to the results of XRD and PSD. When focused on a single point, CQDs were not detected on unmodified GCN (Fig. 4E), while circular shaped CQDs were found on CNQD(180)12. CQDs were shown to be circular shaped with a narrow size distribution ranging between 2 and 5 nm [50]. The inter-planar spacing of CQDs was 0.23 nm, which is in good agreement with other Refs. [51,52].

The samples were further examined by PL spectra and UV–vis spectrometer to study their optical properties. The PL spectrum (Fig. 5 A and B) showed excitation of GCN and CNQDs under irradiation by a laser source (266 nm, 30 mW). The PL intensities decreased as the HT temperature increased from 100 °C to 180 °C (Fig. 5A). In particular, CNQD(180)12 showed a significant decrease of PL intensity. As for the HT time effect (Fig. 5B), GCN had the strongest intrinsic emission band that could be attributed to the fastest recombination between e− and h+ of band transition. When HT treatment was applied, the PL intensities decreased with increases in the HT time. In Fig. S5, GCN(180)12 was also measured for the PL spectra to compare it with GCN and CNQD(180)12. As a result, the HT treatment (for 12 h at 180 °C) for GCN decreased the PL intensity by about 30%. With the incorporation of CQDs, however, the PL intensity was further reduced by about 80%. This could therefore imply that the introduction of CQDs further assists the separation of photogenerated e− from recombining with h+. This effective charge separation is beneficial to improve photocatalytic performance. At a higher HT time (>4h at 180 °C), CNQDs and GCN(180)12 show the emergence of a new emission band at 436 nm. This observation was attributed to in-plane electron-confined effect of the small-sized 2D nanostructure [53] and the introduction of an electron-withdrawing effect by oxygenous groups [54]. On the other hand, not only GCN(180)12, but also CNQD(180)16 and −20 had shoulder PL emissions in the range of <400 nm (UV region) that could be related to electron-trapping effects of structural defects [55]. A high amount of structural defects can act as a shallow trap for photogenerated e−, consequently reducing the PL lifetime as well as weakening the strength of the PL peak [56]. To gain better understanding on the charge separation by CQDs as well as the effect of highly defected GCN sheets toward the electrons trapping, time-resolved PL decay spectra was conducted under UV (470 nm) irradiation to measure the average PL lifetime. The attained wavelengths of time-resolved PL decay were presented in Fig. 5C. The results showed the CNQD(180)12 has slowest PL decay kinetic, followed by GCN and CNQD(180)20. The average PL lifetime for GCN was determined to be 7.8 ns. After addition of CQDs via HT treatment at 12 h, the CNQD(180)20 has shown an increase of average PL lifetime to 10.5 ns. However, when further increasing HT treatment time to 20 h, the average PL lifetime was drastically decreased. These results were well corresponded with the discussion on PL emission.

In Fig. 5D, the photoelectric properties of GCN and CNQDs were analysed by use of UV–Vis DRS. As the HT time increased, stronger absorbance intensities at UV range were observed with a tail extending to visible light absorption (∼450–800 nm). The absorption peaks in the UV region arose from the π-π* transition of delocalized electrons on all photocatalysts. With the presence of CQDs, the addition of aromatic rings on GCN improved the overall UV adsorption. On the other hand, the extended tail into the visible region resulted from the excitation of n electrons from an unsaturated heteroatom group (oxygen) to a π* orbital, known as the n-π* transition. In Fig. S6, the n-π* transition was shown by GCN(180)12 with a weak visible light absorption stemming from the substitution of N atoms by O atoms. Meanwhile, with the incorporation of CQDs, the absorbance intensity was superior compared to GCN and GCN(180)12. Furthermore, as observed, CQDs changed the color of GCN from light yellow to brown (Fig. S7). This feature could help expend light absorbance in the visible light [49]. Bandgap energies for various photocatalysts were calculated via reflectance data using a Tauc plot (Fig. 5D inset). Then, plotting (F(R)hν)1/2 against hν, Kubelka-Munk function F(R) was derived from the following equation:
(2)
F =
(1 -
R
2
)
2R

where R is the absolute reflectance of the layer and hν is the photon energy. The bandgap energies were determined by extrapolating the maximum slope of the curve to the photon energy axis. As a result, GCN had a bandgap of 2.7 eV. When CQDs were incorporated and hydrothermally treated for a longer time, the bandgaps shifted to lower values. For example, CNQD(180)4, −12, and −20 had bandgaps of about 2.55, 2.3, and 2.2 eV, respectively. The shortening of bandgap could be attributed to the upshifted of VB via substitution of N atoms by O atoms [57,58] and the addition of CQDs leading to the formation of more subband states [59]. All the samples show Lorentzian line at g value of 2.003, which is arise from the unpaired electron in the π aromatic ring of GCN [60]. Compared to GCN (Fig. 5E), both CNQDs shows stronger spin intensity, indicating formation of unpaired electrons after HT treatment.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis of GCN and CNQD(180)12 was performed in order to study the interfacial charge transfer and recombination rates of electrons. The transient photocurrent–time curves obtained under ON/OFF UV irradiation (λ > 365 nm) are shown in Fig. 5F. These results clearly show that the photocurrent densities of CNQD(180)12 were 2–3 times higher than those of GCN. This indicates that the presence of CQDs can efficiently boost the interfacial charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of BPA
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed previously, the poor degradation rate by GCN may be attributable to a high recombination of photogenerated electrons and a bulky structure. However, the kinetic degradation rates of BPA increased when the HT time increased to 12 h but decreased at times longer than 12 h. The improvement of photocatalytic activity in CNQD(180)12 could be governed by a few factors. First of all, the particle size reduction caused by thermal exfoliation was proven by spectroscopic analyses such as TEM, XRD, and zetasizer to increase the number of photo-active sites. Second, when the amine group of GCN was oxidized to CO, the electronegativity of the O atom could delocalize electrons, creating an electron rich condition. Third, the reduced bandgap of CNQD(180)12 generated more photo-active electrons under LED (400 nm). Finally, the superior performance of CNQD(180)12 compared to GCN(180)12 in terms of BPA degradation proved that CQDs play an important role as electron transfer intermediators to facilitate charge separation and extend the lifetime of photogenerated electrons. On the other hand, the key factor for the lowered kinetic rates displayed by CNQD(180)16 and CNQD(180)20 could be attributable to the structural defects which lead to the trapping of photogenerated electrons [61]. These defects severely inhibit electrons from transitioning to the GCN surface, hence, inducing rapid e− and h+ recombination and decreasing the lifetime of the electrons [56].

In photocatalytic degradation, the presence of reactive species is very crucial. In general,
OH,
O2 −, and photogenerated h+ are the main reactive species produced during photocatalysis. To study the role of each reactive species, different scavenging chemicals were employed in the kinetic experiments, and their effects on BPA degradation were determined. The investigation on the degradation of BPA by CNQD(180)12 was conducted with the addition of respective scavengers. In the presence of scavengers, it was clearly shown that the degradation capabilities were differently reduced. This means that all reactive species were produced during photocatalysis and involved in the BPA degradation.

Since these reactive species are important to the degradation of BPA, their generation can be predicted through comparing the VB and CB potentials with the standard redox potential (SRP). Considering that the different potentials between VB and CB were denoted as the bandgap, then VB could be calculated according to the following Eq. (3),
(3)
E
CB
=
E
VB
-
E g
where ECB and EVB are the edge potentials of conduction and valence band, respectively, while the bandgap of the photocatalyst is designated as Eg. The Mott-Schottky analysis was conducted to construct electronic structures of GCN, CNQD(180)12 and CNQD(180)20. The flat band potentials (Vfb) of the photocatalysts can be estimated by extrapolating linear fit to 1/C2 = 0 in Fig. S8. Values of CB for these selected photocatalysts were calculated using Eq. (3). All samples show positive slopes, indicating n-type semiconductors where electrons are the majority charge carriers.

Donor densities (Nd) were calculated from the following Eq. (4),
(4)
1
C
2
=
2
e
ε 0
ε r
N d
V -
V
fb
-
kT
e
where C is the capacitance density, e is the elementary charge unit (1.602 × 10−19 Coulombs), Ƹ0 is the permittivity in vacuum (8.854 × 10−12 F m−1), Ƹr is the relative permittivity (4.6) [62], Vfb is the flat band potential, V is the applied potential, k is the Boltzmann’s constant (1.3807 × 10−23 J K−1) and T is the absolute temperature (298 K).

As results, Nd calculated for GCN, CNQD(180)12 and CNQD(180)20 were 2.54 × 1022, 2.82 × 1022 and 2.67 × 1022 cm−3, respectively. The increase of Nd value represents the improved charge carrier transfer [63]. The Nd sequences were in good agreement with the photocatalysis results and other spectrophotometric analyses.

Through obtaining the effective density of states in CB (Nc) values, the positions of Fermi level (EF) relative to the ECB were also determined using the following Eqs. (5) and (6),
(5)
N c
= 2
2 π
m
e
∗
k T
h
2
3 / 2
(6)
E
CB
-
E F
=
kT
e
l n
N c
N d
where h is the Planck’s constant (6.626 × 10−32 J·s), and me * is the effective mass of electron. The position of EF for GCN, CNQD(180)12 and CNQD(180)20 were −0.992, −0.915 and −0.833 eV, respectively. The results suggested that the Ef were positioned below the CB. Especially, CNQD(180)12 has EF shifted upward toward CB, facilitating charge separation at the semiconductor interface. The electronic structures obtained from Mott-Schottky and Tauc plots were depicted in Fig. S9 and tabulated in Table S4.

According to previous studies, the redox potential for SRP of O2/
O2 − and OH−/
OH are −0.046 and +2.7 V vs. NHE, respectively [64]. Therefore, since the ECB (−0.7 V) is more negative than the SRP of O2/
O2 −, the photogenerated e− can react with O2 to form
O2 −. However,
OH cannot be formed through the reaction of photogenerated h+ and OH− because the EVB (+1.6 V) is lower than the SRP of OH−/
OH (+2.7 V). Although the photogenerated h+ cannot produce
OH, the generated h+ still can oxidize the pollutants. As a result, it is generally accepted that the reactive species during the photocatalysis by CNQD(180)12 are h+ and
O2 −. The results of the scavenging effects were depicted in Fig. 6 A. With the addition of BQ (
O2 − scavenger), the degradation of BPA was significantly retarded as the removal percentage reduced from 85.6% (control - without scavenging chemicals) to 10.1%. The presence of TB (a scavenger chemical for
OH) reduced the BPA degradation to 66.5%. According to the above explanation, the VB position of CNQD(180)12 is not suitable for forming
OH through the reaction of photogenerated h+ with a surface OH group or adsorbed water molecules. However, the scavenging effect of TB could be clarified by effective charge separation by the addition of CQDs on GCN. The charge transferring to CQDs could lower the possibility of photogenerated e− recombination. Therefore, more photogenerated e− will be available on the CB, hence, allowing
O2 − further reaction with
OH. The reactions (7)–(9) are shown as below [65].
(7) O2 + e− → 2
O2 −
(8) 2
O2 − + 2e− + 2H+ → 2H2O2
(9) 2H2O2 + 2e− + 2H+ → 2
OH

The observed retardation could lead one to conclude that ·O2 − plays a significant role in BPA degradation. Not only is it capable of degrading BPA, but ·O2 − could possibly produce
OH, which is a very strong oxidation radical. For this reason, the scavenging of
O2 − by BQ also inhibits the production of
OH. Therefore, BQ significantly impacts BPA removal. To further prove the production of
OH, the KI method [66] was adopted to determine the concentration of H2O2. According to Torres et al. [67], the unused
OH will be recombined as H2O2. Consequently, as shown in Fig. 6B, without the addition of BPA, the concentration of H2O2 produced by CNQD(180)12 was 3-times higher than that produced by GCN. Most importantly, the concentration of H2O2 detected was significantly lower in the presence of BPA. Based on this observation, it follows that
OH can be used for the degradation of BPA. As a scavenger for photogenerated h+, TEOA also reduced the BPA degradation capacity by 53%. Based on all these results, the
O2 − has a slightly higher degradation potential than the photogenerated h+. The overall results related to scavenging chemicals and reactive species which responsible for BPA degradation were tabulated in Table S5. Fig. 7 shows the mechanism of BPA degradation according to the electronic structure and scavenging experiments.

The reusability of CNQD(180)12 was conducted in order to determine the stability of the photocatalyst (Fig. 8 ). During the first cycle, 90.4% BPA was removed. Then, for the consecutive cycles, the CNQD(180)12 was recovered by filtration and flushed with distillated water to remove any residual BPA. Degradation percentages decreased by 3.7, 6.4, 10.5, and 11% for cycles 2, 3, 4, and 5, respectively. These slight decreases might be due to non-degradable BPA by-product(s) adsorbed on the h+, consequently reducing the number of reactive sites after each cycle [68].

Altogether, composites of GCN with CQDs prepared through facile HT treatment showed better photocatalytic performance than GCN alone. In particular, CNQD(180)12 had the highest degradation rate (3.0 times higher than GCN) under near visible range LED irradiation. The overall spectroscopic and experimental results indicated that the significant enhancement of photocatalytic activities was attributable to the addition of CQDs into GCN as well as appropriate conditions for hydrothermal oxidation. This related to the better charge separation, higher photo-active sites, substitution of N atom by oxygenated group (creating an electron rich condition), and improved light harvesting by shortening the bandgap. The sequence of main reactive species was
O2 − >
OH > h+. The energy cost efficacy of CNQD(180)12 was compared to other studies was compiled in Table S6 so that it represents the highest kinetic rate per watt (5.6 × 10−4 min−1 W−1). This photocatalyst shows promising prospects, particularly regarding the impressive light harvesting at near-visible range, specifically the activation by a low energy light source (LED), which has longer lifetime and energy cost compared to conventional mercury lamp, allows the operation of an actual water treatment system as a cost-effective approach.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF-2017 R1D1A1B03029441) and Seoul Green Environment Center (SGEC, 17-01-04-02-01) and Research Grant of Kwangwoon University in 2018 for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.12.064.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
